coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of waste soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethane for exploration of its green and various application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yinhui
Zhang, Wenshuo
Deng, Junjie
Zhong, Yuye
Zhao, Pingping
Li, Houbin
Zhang, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Waste soybean oil resulting from repeated cooking and heating is a significant component of food industry waste. Transforming this waste soybean oil into eco-friendly and various products offers an efficient solution to enhance resource utilization. This study employed thermal cycling treatment to simulate the cooking process of soybean oil and analyzed its impact on synthesizing epoxidized soybean oil, soybean oil polyols, and soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethanes. Waste soybean oil was demonstrated to be a cost-effective feedstock for producing vegetable oil-based waterborne polyurethanes. For more diversified applications, the synthesized waterborne polyurethanes were utilized in the preparation of carbon quantum dots, resulting in a diverse range of colors through the incorporation of gardenia pigments. Moreover, through complexing with gelatin, robust composite films and multicolored fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks were produced. The waterborne polyurethane is found to form a composite cross-linking network with gelatin, resulting in an increase in tensile strength of 130.72% and elongation at break of 258.98% for the composite film compared to the pure gelatin film. In comparison to physical blending, chemical grafting of gardenia pigment enhances the printability and firmness of the ink. The printed pattern produced by the ink emits a vivid blue fluorescence when excited at a wavelength of 365 nm, making it valuable for anti-counterfeiting ink applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste soybean oil
Polyurethane
Carbon dots
Composite film
Printing ink

Preparation of soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethane
Synthesis of soybean oil-based carbon quantum dots
Thermal cycle treatment of soybean oil

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant numbers: U1830127). The authors are grateful for constructive feedback from anonymous reviewers that helped improve an earlier version of the manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Appearance of soybean oil after thermal cycling treatment 0 (a), 15 (b), 30 (c) times.
FTIR curves of SBO (a), ESO (b), SOP (c) and S-WPU (d).
FTIR curves of S-CDs.
XRD of S-WPU and S-CDs.
Microstructure of surface (a) and frozen fracture surface (b) of S-WPU film.
SEM of the surface (1) and cross-Section (2) of GS-0 (a)、GS-5 (b)、GSD-5 (c) composite films.
Tensile "stress-strain" curves of GS (a) and GSD (b) composite films.
Effects of the contents of S-WPU(a) and S-CDs(b) on the "UV–visible" transmission curve of the composite film.

Images of 0.5 wt% S-CDs-1(a), S-CDs-2(b) and S-CDs-3(c) dissolved in different solvents under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation. The solvents from left to right are deionized water, EtOH and DCM.
Fluorescence emission-excitation spectra (a) and fluorescence emission-concentration spectra (b) of S-CDs-3.
Images of GSD-W and GSD-G composite inks under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation.
GSD printing inks (a) and their screen printed images (b) on white paper.
Water resistance of GSD-R (a) and GSD-R′ (b) printed patterns.
TGA (a) and DTG (b) curves of GSD composite film.
Epoxidation and ring-opening alcoholization of soybean oil.
Synthesis route of S-WPU and S-CDs.
Preparation scheme of S-CDs.
Composition and proportion of different composite films.
Effects of S-WPU and S-CDs on the mechanical properties of gelatin-based composite films.
Transmittance and opacity of GS and GSD composite films.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of waste soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethane for exploration of its green and various application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nursing, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430071, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nursing, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anqing Normal University, Anqing 246133, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anqing Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anqing Normal University, Anqing 246133, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electronic Information School, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electronic Information School, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Nursing, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430071, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Waste soybean oil resulting from repeated cooking and heating is a significant component of food industry waste. Transforming this waste soybean oil into eco-friendly and various products offers an efficient solution to enhance resource utilization. This study employed thermal cycling treatment to simulate the cooking process of soybean oil and analyzed its impact on synthesizing epoxidized soybean oil, soybean oil polyols, and soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethanes. Waste soybean oil was demonstrated to be a cost-effective feedstock for producing vegetable oil-based waterborne polyurethanes. For more diversified applications, the synthesized waterborne polyurethanes were utilized in the preparation of carbon quantum dots, resulting in a diverse range of colors through the incorporation of gardenia pigments. Moreover, through complexing with gelatin, robust composite films and multicolored fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks were produced. The waterborne polyurethane is found to form a composite cross-linking network with gelatin, resulting in an increase in tensile strength of 130.72% and elongation at break of 258.98% for the composite film compared to the pure gelatin film. In comparison to physical blending, chemical grafting of gardenia pigment enhances the printability and firmness of the ink. The printed pattern produced by the ink emits a vivid blue fluorescence when excited at a wavelength of 365 nm, making it valuable for anti-counterfeiting ink applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing popularity of fried food, the cooking oil utilized for frying has emerged as a significant component of food waste. Repeated heating of cooking oil at high temperatures results in the production of numerous harmful substances. Prolonged consumption of such oil can introduce these substances into the human body, leading to various diseases such as cancer, obesity, heart disease, diabetes, and Alzheimer's disease (Abdo et al., 2023; Szabo et al., 2022). However, directly discarding the oil not only represents a wastage of resources but also contributes to environmental pollution (Fernandez-Silva et al., 2022). For example, 10 million tons of waste oils and fats are produced every year in China, while the effective utilization rate of waste oils and fats is less than 10% (Bai et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2012). Among them, soybean oil accounts for the highest proportion (44%) of edible oil consumption in China in 2023, and its waste volume is huge (Osman et al., 2024; Quevedo-Amador et al., 2024). How to deal with waste soybean oil to achieve green applications represents a crucial concern (Math et al., 2010; Vázquez-Garrido et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2022).

A highly promising approach involves utilizing waste soybean oil to manufacture eco-friendly industrial products, which not only prioritize environmental sustainability but also enhance the value proposition of the products. Waterborne polyurethane exhibits excellent properties such as abrasion resistance, flexibility, corrosion resistance, and processability (Cai et al., 2022; Panda et al., 2018; Patti and Acierno, 2023), which has a wide range of applications in coatings (Mucci et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2024), foams (Caba et al., 2019; Gama et al., 2018; Han et al., 2024; Vásquez et al., 2019), dyes (Chen et al., 2022a, 2023; Kuo et al., 2023), flame retardants (Chen et al., 2022a, 2023; Kuo et al., 2023), food packaging (Kolgesiz et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024a; Zhang et al., 2022a), adhesives (Díez-García et al., 2020; Ren et al., 2021, 2023), inks (Lei et al., 2014; Li et al., 2024b; Zhao et al., 2023), and other fields. Although waterborne polyurethanes decrease reliance on volatile organic solvents, their synthesis still demands substantial quantities of fossil energy as a raw material. In order to further reduce resource consumption, many studies have been conducted to synthesize waterborne polyurethanes using biomass energy sources such as vegetable oils instead of fossil energy sources. Liang et al. prepared waterborne polyurethanes with good mechanical and antimicrobial properties from castor oil and its derivatives using N-methyldiethanolamine as the ionic center (Liang et al., 2018). Zhang et al. developed a series of castor oil-based waterborne polyurethanes with outstanding mechanical properties by controlling the amount of dithiodianiline used in the synthesis of castor oil-based polyurethanes (Zhang et al., 2021). Due to its affordability, ease of acquisition, excellent solvent resistance, and superior safety profile compared to castor oil, soybean oil is more valuable in the synthesis of vegetable oil-based waterborne polyurethanes. Dai et al. prepared fully degradable waterborne polyurethanes from soybean oil, and adjusted the mechanical, thermal and hydrophilic properties of waterborne polyurethane films by varying the R-value (Dai et al., 2020). Chen et al. synthesized self-repairing waterborne polyurethanes using soybean oil as a precursor, employing the Diels-Alder reaction (Chen et al., 2022b). Xiao et al. synthesized a series of waterborne polyurethanes by performing the ring-opening reaction of epoxidized soybean oil, resulting in cured films exhibiting favorable mechanical properties, thermal stability, and multifunctionality (Xiao et al., 2023). However, there is a scarcity of research on waterborne polyurethanes utilizing waste soybean oil, and a lack of studies investigating the impact of thermal processing on the quality of soybean oil products. Therefore, it is a meaningful topic to explore a route to synthesize economic, green and environmentally friendly "waste soybean oil-waterborne polyurethane" using waste soybean oil as raw material.

In addition, it is also worthy of attention to realize more diversified application of the "waste soybean oil-waterborne polyurethane" route. As a biomass material with high availability and biocompatibility, gelatin has a wide range of applications in industries such as printing and packaging (Luo et al., 2022). Gelatin can be extracted from fish skin, avoiding health and religious issues (Huang et al., 2019). Waterborne polyurethane exhibits favorable dispersibility, excellent adhesion, low viscosity, intermodification, and film-forming ability at room temperature (Liu et al., 2020; Si and Zhao, 2021), making it suitable as a binder for waterborne inks. Zhu et al. developed a networked waterborne polyurethane dispersion specifically designed for waterborne inks, leading to significant enhancements in ink adhesion and water resistance (Zhu et al., 2019). Waterborne polyurethanes can serve as excellent precursors for the synthesis of CDs. Gu et al. successfully produced water-soluble CDs through one-pot hydrothermal treatment of waterborne polyurethane emulsions (Gu et al., 2016). Qiang et al. incorporated CDs into waterborne polyurethanes, resulting in composite films exhibiting favorable transparency and photoluminescent properties (Qiang et al., 2020). Zainuddin, F et al. introduced vegetable pigments into polyurethane foams and greatly enhanced the color of the foams (Zainuddin et al., 2018). These studies offer novel insights for expanding the high-value applications of waterborne polyurethanes based on soybean oil.

In this work, waste soybean oil (SBO) was simulated and prepared through the repeated heating of edible soybean oil. Subsequently, epoxidized soybean oil (ESO) was synthesized based on the prepared SBO. Soybean oil polyol (SOP) was then obtained by reacting SBO with small molecule alcohols. Finally, soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethane (S-WPU) were synthesized. FTIR characterization was conducted to investigate the impact of the number of heating cycles on the quality of soybean oil and its effect on the properties of the synthesized ESO. Three carbon quantum dots (S-CDs) based on soybean oil were synthesized using a straightforward hydrothermal/solvent-thermal method, with soybean oil, soybean oil/urea, and S-WPU serving as precursors. The functional group structures of the S-CDs were characterized by FTIR, and their microscopic morphologies, solvent dependence, quantum yields, and fluorescence spectra were examined. Diverse colors of S-WPU were synthesized by incorporating gardenia red, yellow, blue, and green pigments. Additionally, composite films (GSD) and waterborne fluorescent anticounterfeiting ink (GSD ink) were prepared by incorporating S-CDs. The effects of S-WPU and S-CD contents on the mechanical properties of the GSD composite film were analyzed and investigated. Furthermore, the printing effects of GSD ink, prepared using gardenia pigments through chemical grafting and physical blending, were compared.

Edible soybean oil (SBO) was purchased from Harbin Zhong'an Oil Manufacturing Co. Glacial acetic acid (HAC, AR), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), dichloromethane (DCM, 98%), tetrafluoroboric acid (HBF4, AR), acetone (AC, AR), isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, AR), sodium chloride (NaCl, AR), ethanol (EtOH, AR), n-propanol (CH3(CH2)2OH, AR), n-butanol (CH3(CH2)3OH, AR), n-pentanol (CH3(CH2)4OH, AR), 2,2-dihydroxymethyl propionic acid (DMPA, AR), urea (Urea, AR), gelatin (GA, CP), glycerol (GL, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Dibutyltin dilaurate (DBTDL, AR) was purchased from Anegi Chemicals. Triethylamine (TEA, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Myriad Chemical Technology Co. Gardenia pigment (GP) was purchased from Shaanxi Hao Yang Biotechnology Co. Petroleum ether (PE, AR) was purchased from McLean Reagent Co. Foam-breaking polysiloxane solution (BYK, 98%), and polyethylene wax paste (PE-WAX, 98%) were purchased from Greenlink Chemical Technology Co. AC was treated with potassium permanganate overnight prior to use, distilled under reduced pressure, and stored sealed for backup. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulated generation of waste soybean oil
ce:section.ce:para: SBO (500 mL) was heated at 250 ℃ for 10 min and continuously stirred in a glass beaker. The heat treatment was repeated to simulate the frying process during cooking. SBO that have been heat treated 0/15/30 times were labeled SBO-0, SBO-15, and SBO-30, respectively

To prepare the epoxide reagent (ER), HAC (36.60 g), H2O2 (100.00 g), H2SO4 (2.00 g) were mixed in a flask in sequence and magnetically stirred at room temperature for 10 min, then sealed at 4 ℃. The simulated waste SBO (100.00 g) was heated to 65 ℃ and continuously stirred magnetically, and then ER was added to the reaction system drop by drop. The reaction continues for 30 min to obtain a uniform golden opaque liquid. After the system is brought to room temperature, a saturated NaHCO3 solution is added to remove the acid. After that, the mixed system was fully washed with NaCl solution and DCM. Epoxidized soybean oil (ESO) is obtained after spin steaming, vacuum distillation and sealed storage. ESO prepared from SBO-0, SBO-15 and SBO-30 were labeled as ESO-0, ESO-15 and ESO-30, respectively.

10.00 g of different alcohols (ethanol, n-propanol, n-butanol, n-amyl alcohol) were dissolved in DCM and cooled to −3 ℃, and 0.2 mL of HBF4 was added and fully magnetically stirred. After 10 min, ESO (10.00 g) was slowly added to the system and continued to react. After 10 min, a sufficient amount of deionized water was added and continuously stirred at room temperature to terminate the reaction. After that, the mixed system was fully washed with NaCl solution and DCM. Soybean oil polyol (SOP) is obtained after spin steaming, vacuum distillation and sealed storage. SOP prepared with ethanol, n-propanol, n-butanol and n-amyl alcohol as raw materials were labeled as SOP-1, SOP-2, SOP-3 and SOP-4, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethane
ce:section.ce:para: SOP (3.50 g) is distilled under reduced pressure to remove water and placed in a three-necked flask. At room temperature, IPDI (1.05 g) was added and refluxed at 85 ℃ for 2 h. DMPA (0.40 g) was added to continue the reaction for 2 h, and AC was used to adjust the viscosity of the reaction system. Subsequently, 0.2 wt% DBTDL was added as a catalyst and continued to react for 30 min. After the reaction system was reduced to room temperature, TEA (1.05 g) was added and stirred for 30 min. After that, deionized water was added and mechanically stirred (1200 r/min, 15 min) to obtain a white transparent soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethane (S-WPU) emulsion with a solid content of 20 wt%. Further, in order to prepare polychromatic S-WPU, GP (0.05 g) was used to replace equal quality DMPA and other experimental procedures remained unchanged. Soybean oil-based waterborne polyurethanes were obtained in blue (S-WPU-B), red (S-WPU-R), yellow (S-WPU-Y) and green (S-WPU-G), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of soybean oil-based carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: S-CDs was prepared by hydrothermal/solvothermal method. The precursors and synthesis conditions of three S-CDs are shown in Table 1. After filtration, chromatography, rotary steaming, dialysis and freeze-drying, the products were sealed and stored and labeled as S-CDs-1, S-CDs-2 and S-CDs-3 respectively.

GA, S-WPU, S-CDs and GL were mixed according to the formula described in Table 2, and dried for 12 h in a blast drying oven at 40 ℃ to obtain GS and GSD composite films. For composite inks, 1 wt% of the defoamer was added and the drying process was canceled (Zhang et al., 2022b).

The molecular structures of SBO, ESO, SOP, S-WPU and S-CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The test was carried out on a Nicolet 5700FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, Waltham, MA) and the scanning range was 4000–500 cm−1.
The acid and epoxy values of ESO and SOP, and the hydroxyl values of SOP were tested and calculated according to existing methods (Westerman et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2015).

The viscosity changes of SOP in the range of 25–65 ℃ were measured by Brinell viscometer NDJ-8S. This instrument is a digital viscometer, driven by the motor through the variable speed rotor for constant speed rotation. The viscous moment acting on the rotor is detected by the sensor, and the viscosity of the measured liquid is obtained after processing by the computer.

An X-ray diffractometer (XRD) instrument (D8-A25 Advances, Bruker-axs) was used to analyze the crystallinity of S-WPU and S-CDs. The range of angle was 2–80°. The scanning rate was 5°/min. Further, S-WPU, GS and GSD composite films were freeze-fractured in liquid nitrogen and sprayed with gold by plasma sputtering. The cross-section and the surface morphology of films were characterized under high vacuum mode at 5 kV on a field emission scanning electron microscope (AMBER GMH, Czech/ TESCAN). Images were captured at 5000 × magnification.

A UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Ultrospec 2000; Scinteck, UK) was used to characterize the transmittance of GS, GSD composite films. The incident light wavelength ranges from 200 to 700 nm. The transmittance at 600 nm is used to calculate the opacity of the GS, GSD composite films according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Opacity
1 / mm
= − log
T
600
/ t

T
600 represents the transmittance at 600 nm and t represents the average thickness of the GS, GSD composite films (mm).
The fluorescence spectrum of S-CDs waterborne solution was tested on a fluorescence spectrometer F-7000 (Hitachi, Japan), and the excitation wavelength range was 350–450 nm.

A digital micrometer (Model IP65, SAMA Tools, Viareggio, Italy) was used to measure the average thickness of the composite film by calculating the average of the thicknesses of five random points on the film. Then, the GS, GSD composite films were cut into standard dumbbell-shaped specimens of 25 ×5 mm and tested on universal tensile testing machine (Instron 3343, USA) at a stretching speed of 100 mm/min.

The thermal analysis test of S-WPU, GS and GSD composite films was carried out by thermogravimetry (TG209 F1 Libra thermal balance). The sample masses were 5–10 mg. The temperature range is 25–600 ℃, the heating rate is 10 ℃/min, and the nitrogen rate is 20 mL/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal cycle treatment of soybean oil
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows that the color of soybean oil becomes lighter and then darker as the number of heating increases. Before heating, it is a transparent and clear golden yellow color, and when heated up to 15 times, it becomes light yellow, and then the color becomes darker and deeper dramatically, and finally it becomes a viscous reddish-brown liquid. This is because as heating proceeds, natural pigments such as lutein, carotenoids and carotenoids oxidize and decompose, causing the soybean oil to lighten in color. Subsequently, more times and intense heating causes triglycerides, phospholipids and other components to begin to denature, producing trans fatty acids, benzopyrene, fatty acid polymers and even tiny carbon particles, resulting in a darker, blacker color and a much higher viscosity (Jin et al., 2021; Vázquez-Garrido et al., 2023).

The FTIR spectrum curve confirms the process of oxidation and deterioration of SBO by heating ( Fig. 2a). With the increase of heating times, the absorption band of SBO around 3500 cm−1 gradually weakened, indicating that -OH and -COOH were partially destroyed. The single bond vibration absorption peak of γ-OH at 750–650 cm−1 gradually disappeared, indicating that a low molecular weight oil polymer may be formed, which can be proved by the changes of CC and -CO absorption peaks around 1650 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1. The weakened peak intensity shows that the content of unsaturated fatty acids decreases, and the gradually widened CC absorption peak shows that the proportion of molecular association states increases. The absorption peak of -CH2 at 2950 cm−1 became weaker but sharper, indicating that some molecular chains were broken to form small molecular aliphatic hydrocarbons. It can be found that heating can make the molecular chain in soybean oil oxidize, break and oligomerize at the same time, but the main functional groups still remain, which contributes to the molecular structure basis for exploring the reuse of waste soybean oil.

The synthesis principle of ESO and SOP was shown in Scheme 1. In the process of epoxidation, H2O2 peroxides HAC to form peroxy acid. Due to the catalysis of H2SO4, CC forms epoxy group under the action of peroxy acid to obtain ESO (Meadows et al., 2018). In the process of epoxy ring opening, the -OH of monohydric alcohol generates active center under the action of HBF4, which attacks the epoxy group in ESO, so that the epoxy group opens and forms C-O-C with the active center, and the active center moves to the end and attacks the new epoxy group (Santacesaria et al., 2020). After the reaction, the hydroxyl group was terminated under the action of water to obtain SOP.

Fig. 2(b) shows the effect of SBO heating treatment on the molecular structure of ESO. The absorption peaks of three ESO all appeared at 850 cm−1, and compared with the FTIR curve of SBO, the absorption peak of CC disappeared completely, and the absorption peak of C-O-C became the strongest, which proved the success of epoxidation. In addition, the characteristic peaks of -OH (3500 cm−1), -CH2 (2950 cm−1), -CO (1500 cm−1) and C-O-C (1110 cm−1) were found in the FTIR curves of three ESO. Differently, the curve of ESO-15 and ESO-30 show slight hybrid peaks in the range of 1500–1000 cm−1, which comes from the trace small molecular impurities generated during the heat treatment process. Overall, the impurities did not affect the overall structure and main functional groups of ESO, which proved that the use of waste soybean oil to prepare ESO is feasible. In comparison, the yield of ESO-30 is 14.85% lower than that of ESO-0, and the epoxide value is reduced by 0.11 mol/100 g (Table S1). The reduction in product quality is not significant, and the preparation of ESO from waste soybean oil is a more economical option considering the overall value of waste biomass recycling.

Fig. 2(c) demonstrates the FTIR spectra of SOP came from ESO-30. Compared with the FTIR spectral profile of ESO, the C-O-C absorption peaks of SOP still existed, while the epoxy group absorption peaks appearing near 850 cm−1 were significantly weakened to almost disappeared, proving the success of the ring-opening polymerization reaction. It can be noticed that the smaller the molecular weight of the alcohol used, the more epoxy functional groups are consumed and the weaker the epoxy peak of the synthesized SOP. In addition, the hydroxyl group of the polyol exhibited a broad absorption band near 3500 cm−1. The FTIR curves of the four groups of SOP are also basically consistent, which is attributed to the fact that the growth of the carbon chains of the monohydric alcohols used as raw materials is not obvious and does not cause significant negative effects on the molecular structure of the SOP. The effect of small molecule alcohols on SOP is reflected in the macroscopic index of the product (Table S2, Fig. S1). With the extension of the carbon chain, the yield of SOP decreased and the viscosity increased significantly. The epoxy, hydroxyl and acid values remained essentially unchanged. The decrease in yield is attributed to the decrease in reactivity of the long chain alcohols, but the decrease is acceptable (4.49%). The increase in viscosity is attributed to the significant increase in molecular weight. Thus, SOP with different viscosities can be prepared by simply changing the type of small molecule alcohol to meet different product requirements.

The synthesis principle of S-WPU and S-CDs was shown in Scheme 2. The FTIR curve of S-WPU is shown in Fig. 2(d). Compared with SOP, S-WPU retained the absorption bands and peaks at 3600–3200 cm−1, 2900 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1, which were attributed to -OH, -CH2 and C-O-C, respectively, which proved that the basic structure in the main chain of SOP was retained. Meanwhile, two obvious absorption peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1 were added in S-WPU, which were attributed to -CO and N-H, respectively, and both of them, together with C-O-C, constituted the signature functional group of polyurethanes, -RNHCOOR'-, which proved that polyurethanes were successfully synthesized.

The FTIR curves of S-CDs are shown in Fig. 3. It can be found that the functional group compositions of the CDs synthesized based on the three different methods are quite different. The similarities among the three are the presence of absorption peaks at 3700–3200 cm−1, 2900 cm−1, 1680 cm−1, and 1100 cm−1, which are attributed to -OH, -CH2, -CO, and C-O-C, respectively. However, in contrast, the -OH absorption bands of S-CDs-2 and S-CDs-3 are obviously stronger and wider than those of S-CDs-1. This proves that the -OH of S-CDs-2 and S-CDs-3 is denser, which brings stronger hydrogen bonding, and the microcomposition of bound states contributes to the broadening of the absorption bands. This change suggests that the introduction of Urea and IPDI is more favorable to the formation of carbon nuclei in CDs, thus contributing to the concentrated distribution of -OH on the surface of carbon nuclei. Near 1100 cm−1, the C-O-C in S-CDs-1 should come from the autoxidation of CC during the hydrothermal process of soybean oil. The intensity of the C-O-C peaks of S-CDs-2 is significantly weaker than that of the other two groups, which proves that the formation of CDs becomes easier in the presence of Urea. It is noteworthy that the absorption peak of S-CDs-2 at 1450 cm−1 is significantly stronger, which proves that the C-N content is significantly increased and the introduction of Urea plays a role of nitrogen doping. The absorption peak at 800 cm−1 in S-CDs-1 is derived from the aromatic ring structure produced during high heat treatment of soybean oil. Such small molecules may be the structural basis for the fluorescent effect of S-CDs-1. In contrast, S-CDs-3 has a balanced distribution and content of various functional groups, which may lead to a more stable fluorescence effect. Its very broad absorption band at -OH proves that the CDs tend to polymerize with each other.

The XRD curves of S-WPU and S-CDs are shown in Fig. 4. S-WPU is amorphous structure and the main crystal diffraction band is wide, and the strongest diffraction angle appears around 19.8°, which comes from the hard segment arrangement of -RNHCOOR'-. Similar to S-WPU, the three groups of S-CDs have no obvious diffraction peaks, which proves that S-CDs belong to Carbonized polymer dots with carbon core but no obvious lattice configuration. In contrast, the diffraction peak intensity of S-CDs-3 is higher, which proves that the introduction of aliphatic ring in IPDI is more conducive to the formation of carbon nuclei. On the other hand, the XRD curve of S-CDs-2 has a weak peak near 22.4°. This comes from the C-N structure in Urea. Therefore, Urea is an effective precursor for doping nitrogen into soybean oil, which may optimize the fluorescence emission of S-CDs.

For waterborne polyurethane, microphase separation structure is the most important index affecting its mechanical properties. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the surface of S-WPU is uniform and compact, and there are no visible bulges, defects or caking under 1000 times magnification irradiation, which proves that the molecular chains of S-WPU can be uniformly crosslinked to form a uniform and stable film. The microstructure of the frozen fracture surface of S-WPU is shown in Fig. 5(b). It can be found that under the magnification of 1000 times, there is an obvious scale-like distribution structure of "bright lines-dark color blocks" on the fracture surface. A large number of studies show that bright areas are the main brittle fracture sites, representing the aggregation of hard segments, while dark areas represent the aggregation of soft segments of polyols. Therefore, S-WPU has obvious microphase separation structure of soft and hard segments. Due to the high hydroxyl value content of polyols and dendritic distribution, the distribution of hard segments is dense, which is beneficial to improve the mechanical strength of polyurethane.

Fig. 6 further shows the micro-morphology of the "gelatin-waterborne polyurethane" composite film. GS-0, GS-5 and GSD-5 were selected as representative samples. As shown in Fig. 6(a1-c1), the surfaces of the three groups of composite membranes are dense, but the flatness of GSD-5 surface is low, which is due to the different surface water loss rates caused by the agglomeration of S-CDs on the surface and inside the material. However, as shown in Fig. 6(a2-c2), both S-WPU and S-CDs will obviously affect the cross-sectional morphology of the composites. After adding S-WPU, the fracture surface of GS-5 has obvious microphase separation structure and a large number of pores, which proves that the entanglement of GA and S-WPU molecular chains is loose. This is because S-WPU with soybean oil as precursor has certain hydrophobicity. Interestingly, after S-CDs was added, the holes in the fracture surface disappeared and the microphase separation structure became blurred, which indicated that S-CDs had affinity for both GA and S-WPU, and could be used as a common connection site to optimize the molecular chain cross-linking structure through hydrogen bonding, making the composite system more compact.

Fig. 7 and Table 3 show the effects of S-WPU and S-CDs on the mechanical properties of the composite membrane. It can be found that S-WPU is extremely flexible, and the elongation at break is 357.01%, which is higher than that of pure gelatin film. With the increase of S-WPU content, the elongation at break of GS gradually increased, while the tensile strength first increased and then decreased, reaching the highest value of 16.89 MPa at GS-3. The improvement of elongation at break comes from the flexibility of S-WPU, and the improvement of tensile strength comes from the formation of intertwined composite molecular chains. The hydrogen bonding between molecular chains will improve the mechanical strength. However, when the content of S-WPU is too high, the entanglement of molecular chains becomes loose (Fig. 6 b2), and the strength of the material decreases.

The effect of S-CDs on the composite films is different from that of S-WPU (Fig. 7b). With the increase of S-CDs content, the tensile strength of the composite film gradually increased, and the elongation at break first increased and then decreased, reaching the maximum value at GSD-5 (30.25 MPa) and GSD-2 (309.61%), respectively. This is because proper distribution of S-CDs can disperse stress in GSD, but the increase of excessive S-CDs will lead to carbon dots reunion (Fig. 6 c2), resulting in stress concentration and deteriorated flexibility. On the whole, the best comprehensive mechanical properties of S-WPU and S-CDs were obtained at 30 wt% and 0.5 wt% respectively.

Fig. 8 and Table 4 show the optical properties of GS and GSD composite films. The introduction of S-WPU has no significant effect on the transparency of the composite film (Fig. 8a). When the addition of S-WPU reaches 50 wt%, the transmittance at 600 nm still reaches 80.05%, which is only 6.58% lower than that of pure GA film. Compared with GSD-0, the transmittance of GSD-5 at 600 nm decreased by 17.68% and the opacity increased by 146.43% (Fig. 8b). Because CDs itself belongs to color-developing nanoparticles, this decrease in opacity is inevitable, but on the whole, GSD composite film still has high light transmittance (> 65%), which can meet the needs of daily packaging film materials.

The influence of solvent types on the fluorescence emission effect of S-CDs is shown in Fig. 9. It can be found that S-CDs-1 can be dissolved in EtOH and DCM, but the dissolution rate in water is almost zero after ultrasonic treatment. This is because S-CDs-1 is completely treated with soybean oil as a precursor, and has almost no dispersibility in water. In contrast, both S-CDs-2 and S-CDs-3 formed transparent and stable waterborne solutions, which proved that hydrophilic groups were introduced into them. S-CDs-2 and S-CDs-3 can also be dissolved in organic solvents to obtain a transparent and uniform light yellow solution.

Under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, the solution of S-CDs-1 emits blue fluorescence, and both S-CDs-2 and S-CDs-3 emit blue fluorescence. In addition, the fluorescence of S-CDs-1 is grayish white, which proves that the fluorescence emission peaks of S-CDs-1 are miscellaneous. Compared with S-CDs-2 and S-CDs-3, the fluorescence brightness of S-CDs-1 is obviously lower. Further comparing S-CDs-2 with S-CDs-3, it is found that the fluorescence brightness of S-CDs-2 is higher than that of S-CDs-3 in organic solvent, but in water, the fluorescence brightness of S-CDs-3 is higher than that of S-CDs-2. This is because S-CDs-2 contains small molecule carbon dots, which can obtain higher fluorescence efficiency in organic solvents with higher solubility. However, S-CDs-3 belongs to carbonized polymer dot with low average carbonization degree, and there are a large number of fluorescent small molecular groups in the polymer-like structure. In waterborne solution, fluorescent small molecular groups are confined by carbonized polymer skeleton and can emit strong fluorescence. However, in organic solvents with higher solubility, small fluorescent groups dissociate from the carbonized polymer skeleton, which is easier to be quenched and reduces the overall fluorescence emission effect.

Since the subsequent gelatin/waterborne polyurethane systems all use water as solvent, S-CDs-3 was selected for fluorescence spectrum analysis. It can be found from Fig. 10(a) that the optimal emission wavelength of S-CDs-3 is near 455 nm, which is blue fluorescence. The optimum excitation wavelength is around 410 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of S-CDs shows a certain excitation dependence, which is the universality of polymer carbon dots. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission spectrum shows a slight red shift, and the shift of secondary emission peak is more obvious. From Fig. 10(b), it can be found that the strongest fluorescence emission is obtained when the concentration is 0.05 wt% in waterborne solution, and the fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of S-CDs-3 have no obvious concentration dependence.

Fig. 11 shows the fluorescent anti-counterfeiting effect of GSD ink. It can be found that GSD ink can emit bright blue-green fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet light, and the fluorescence pattern is clear and recognizable. When printing such a complex pattern as QR code, the fluorescence image will be distorted to some extent, but the pattern itself can meet the requirements of mobile phone scanning and recognition. In addition, the color of the ink itself will have a certain impact on the color of fluorescence. For example, when GSD-G ink is used, the overall visual effect of fluorescent ink will be green, but this is the result of fluorescence illuminating the ink and does not hinder the emission wavelength of fluorescence itself. On the whole, both white and green GSD inks can show good fluorescence emission effect under ultraviolet irradiation, which has certain anti-counterfeiting application value.

The appearance and printing effect of five kinds of GSD composite inks were observed and tested. The appearance of GSD inks is shown in Fig. 12(a). GSD ink presents a uniform and stable emulsion state, with low transparency but good dispersibility, and no obvious precipitation after long-term storage. The printing effect of GSD inks is shown in Fig. 12(b). The patterns in all five colors have an even color distribution, stable shapes, and clear boundaries. The Lab values and color difference values of the printed pattern colors are shown in the Table S3, indicating clear colors and obvious color differences. It proves that GSD is a good water-based ink suitable for multi-color printing on the surface of paper products.

Fig. 13 shows the influence of whether GP is introduced into WPU in the form of grafting on the printing fastness of ink. It can be found that directly mixing gardenia pigment into ink in the form of physical dispersion makes the printed pattern more vivid, because when gardenia pigment is added into WPU as a chain extender, its chromophore is interfered by molecular chains, which makes the color fade. However, GP is very soluble in water, and when it is wiped with a wet towel, the loss of pigment is obvious. After ten wipes, the printed pattern of GSD-R′ became blurred and almost indistinguishable (Fig. 13b). On the other hand, GSD-R is basically clear and intact because gardenia pigment remains stable in S-WPU-R molecular chain, although the pattern is also lost to some extent. Therefore, introducing natural pigments rich in -OH into WPU is a good scheme to improve the printability and printfastness of water-soluble pigments in humid environment. In addition, the printing effect of GSD inks on a variety of printing substrates was comparatively studied (Fig. S2). The printed patterns on paper, PET film and glass are all complete in content and clear in shape. After being continuously scratched by rigid cardboard, the printed pattern remained clear and intact, proving good abrasion resistance.

The thermal decomposition process of GSD is shown in Fig. 14. It can be found that the first weight loss peak of the composite membrane appears near 120 ℃, which comes from the loss of structural water. The weight loss peak of GS-0 is obviously wider than that of other composite films, which is because gelatin has stronger water-absorbing ability. The main weight loss peak of the composite film appears around 320 ℃, which is attributed to the thermal degradation of polypeptide chains in gelatin and soft segments in S-WPU. Compared with GS-0, S-WPU and composite membrane have a new weight loss peak at 250 ℃, which comes from the break of urethane bond. At 600 ℃, the mass residue of GS-0 is about 22.57%, which is obviously higher than other composite membranes. This is because S-WPU contains more elements of H, N and O, which is more conducive to the formation of gaseous pyrolysis products of C than solid residues. Generally speaking, due to the low heat resistance of soybean oil, S-WPU will reduce the heat resistance of composites to some extent, but the overall initial decomposition temperature is still above 130 ℃, while the ambient temperature of printing industry is usually around room temperature and the temperature of common pasteurization method is also below 100 ℃. Therefore, on the whole, the thermal stability of GSD meets the basic requirements of industrial environment.

In this work, a complete preparation route from waste soybean oil to bio-based composite film and composite ink was put forward. The simulated waste SBO was successfully used to synthesize ESO and SPO. The number of heat treatments is proved not to have a significant adverse effect on the product quality. SOP was further used to obtain waterborne polyurethane and carbon dots. S-WPU has excellent microphase separation structure and high flexibility. S-CDs exhibits fluorescence emission performance with wide solvent adaptability. S-WPU and S-CDs can be used as excellent reinforcements of gelatin-based materials, and the tensile strength and elongation at break are increased by 130.72% and 258.98% respectively. The transparency of gelatin and the fluorescence reflection performance of S-CDs are maintained in the composite film/ink. Furthermore, natural gardenia pigment was introduced into S-WPU to obtain multicolor ink, which showed good stability, printability and water resistance. Overall, this work provides a new scheme for the high-value industrial application of waste soybean oil.

Yuye Zhong: Validation, Formal analysis. Junjie Deng: Resources, Formal analysis. Houbin Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Pingping Zhao: Validation, Investigation. Wenshuo Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yinhui Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Tao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Yinhui Zhang and Wenshuo Zhang contributed equally to this work. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant numbers: U1830127). The authors are grateful for constructive feedback from anonymous reviewers that helped improve an earlier version of the manuscript.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Santacesaria et al., 2020
